structured.summary: This paper proposes FewGen, a method for few-shot learning that first tunes an autoregressive pretrained language model (PLM) with a meta-weighted maximum likelihood objective to generate label-discriminative synthetic training samples, which are then used to augment the original few-shot dataset for classifier fine-tuning. The approach improves performance on seven GLUE classification tasks compared to existing few-shot and augmentation methods.

strengths.point: Novel meta-weighted maximum likelihood objective for generator tuning that emphasizes label-discriminative tokens.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.2 details the meta-weighted training objective and Algorithm 1 describes the training procedure.

strengths.point: Effective use of prefix-tuning to adapt the generator PLM on few-shot data, reducing memory and overfitting issues.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.1 and 3.2 discuss prefix-tuning benefits and implementation.

strengths.point: Robust classifier fine-tuning procedure using temporal ensembling to mitigate label noise from generated samples.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.3 and Algorithm 2 describe the noise-robust fine-tuning approach.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation showing FewGen outperforms strong baselines including GPT3Mix despite using a much smaller generator model.
strengths.evidence: Section 5.1 and Table 1 present results across seven GLUE tasks with significant improvements.

weaknesses.point: The method requires tuning an additional large generator model, increasing computational cost and complexity.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6 discusses higher computation costs and longer running time compared to direct few-shot fine-tuning.

weaknesses.point: Limited analysis on the diversity and linguistic quality of generated samples beyond label discriminativeness.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.3 and Table 3 focus mainly on label discriminativeness and fluency but do not quantify diversity or other quality metrics.

weaknesses.point: The approach relies on the availability of a development set of the same size as the training set for meta-learning, which may not always be feasible.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 states the strict few-shot setting with D_dev of same size as D_train used for meta-objective.

weaknesses.point: The method is evaluated only on GLUE classification tasks; generalization to other NLP tasks or generation settings is not demonstrated.
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments in Section 5 focus exclusively on seven GLUE classification tasks.

weaknesses.point: The impact of hyperparameter choices, such as the weighting network architecture and temporal ensembling parameters, is not extensively explored.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4 and Appendix B mention hyperparameter settings but lack ablation or sensitivity analysis.

weaknesses.point: The method assumes uniform label prior in the meta-objective, which may limit applicability to imbalanced datasets.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.2 mentions uniform label prior assumption in the discriminative loss formulation.

weaknesses.point: Potential ethical risks related to bias and misinformation generation are acknowledged but not empirically studied or mitigated.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6 discusses ethical considerations but does not provide experiments or mitigation strategies.

questions.question: How sensitive is FewGen to the size and quality of the development set used for meta-learning the token weights?
questions.motivation: Understanding this is important for practical scenarios where a development set of equal size to training data may not be available.

questions.question: Can the authors provide quantitative metrics or analyses on the diversity and novelty of the generated samples beyond label discriminativeness?
questions.motivation: Diversity and novelty are critical for effective data augmentation and generalization.

questions.question: How does FewGen perform on tasks with imbalanced label distributions or multi-label settings?
questions.motivation: The current assumption of uniform label prior may limit applicability to real-world datasets with imbalance.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or experimented with jointly training the generator and classifier models to further improve augmentation quality and classification performance?
questions.motivation: Joint training could potentially leverage mutual feedback and improve overall results.

critique_points.point_001: The method requires tuning an additional large generator model, increasing computational cost and complexity. (Section 6 discusses higher computation costs and longer running time compared to direct few-shot fine-tuning.)
critique_points.point_002: Limited analysis on the diversity and linguistic quality of generated samples beyond label discriminativeness. (Section 5.3 and Table 3 focus mainly on label discriminativeness and fluency but do not quantify diversity or other quality metrics.)
critique_points.point_003: The approach relies on the availability of a development set of the same size as the training set for meta-learning, which may not always be feasible. (Section 3.1 states the strict few-shot setting with D_dev of same size as D_train used for meta-objective.)
critique_points.point_004: The method is evaluated only on GLUE classification tasks; generalization to other NLP tasks or generation settings is not demonstrated. (Experiments in Section 5 focus exclusively on seven GLUE classification tasks.)
critique_points.point_005: The impact of hyperparameter choices, such as the weighting network architecture and temporal ensembling parameters, is not extensively explored. (Section 4 and Appendix B mention hyperparameter settings but lack ablation or sensitivity analysis.)
critique_points.point_006: The method assumes uniform label prior in the meta-objective, which may limit applicability to imbalanced datasets. (Section 3.2 mentions uniform label prior assumption in the discriminative loss formulation.)
critique_points.point_007: Potential ethical risks related to bias and misinformation generation are acknowledged but not empirically studied or mitigated. (Section 6 discusses ethical considerations but does not provide experiments or mitigation strategies.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is FewGen to the size and quality of the development set used for meta-learning the token weights? (Understanding this is important for practical scenarios where a development set of equal size to training data may not be available.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the authors provide quantitative metrics or analyses on the diversity and novelty of the generated samples beyond label discriminativeness? (Diversity and novelty are critical for effective data augmentation and generalization.)
critique_points.point_010: How does FewGen perform on tasks with imbalanced label distributions or multi-label settings? (The current assumption of uniform label prior may limit applicability to real-world datasets with imbalance.)
critique_points.point_011: Have the authors considered or experimented with jointly training the generator and classifier models to further improve augmentation quality and classification performance? (Joint training could potentially leverage mutual feedback and improve overall results.)
